note: Cumulative ledger of every use case that ever passed the quality bar. The public top 10 is a moving window over this list.

items.title: One-prompt audit of an entire knowledge vault with parallel agents
items.why_useful: A single prompt sent 8 audit agents through 13GB of a personal knowledge vault, reviewing every automation and Claude Code skill built since January. A ready-made recipe for periodically auditing any large agentic setup instead of trusting it blindly.
items.author: tomcrawshaw01

items.title: The Auditor pattern: a fresh session that distrusts the agent's own memory
items.why_useful: An AI trading agent declared itself profitable; an audit by Fable 5 in a brand-new session with the instruction 'don't trust the memory's conclusions, read the data' found a loss instead. The transferable idea: every working agent needs an independent auditor with no shared history.
items.author: c_neumann20

items.title: Tell the orchestrator explicitly which model its subagents must use
items.why_useful: Fable 5 loves spawning subagents, and by default they can run on the expensive tier. The fix is one line in your prompt: 'subagents should use Opus/Sonnet'. Corroborated by multiple users converging on 'Fable plans, cheaper models execute'.
items.author: RickTDing

items.title: Real-world burn rates: 107 subagents and 975k tokens in 10 minutes
items.why_useful: Concrete cost anecdotes from day one: 107 agents spawned in 10 minutes burning 975k tokens, and a small half-hour project consuming a full 5-hour usage limit. Useful calibration data before pointing Fable 5 at anything unattended.
items.author: thejordanwood
items.claimed_metrics: 107 agents, 975k tokens in 10 min (self-reported by author)

items.title: Measure cost per thinking-effort level with a fixed benchmark artifact
items.why_useful: Simon Willison generated the same artifact at every thinking-effort level and published the API cost of each, plus a TIL on computing token spend for a model not yet in pricing databases. A replicable method for building your own effort-level rate card.
items.author: simonw

items.title: Harness beats model: the environment is still the multiplier
items.why_useful: On everyday spreadsheet tasks Fable 5 barely moves the needle, while a purpose-built harness and environment dramatically outperform the default skill. Evidence that investing in your tooling around the model keeps paying off even after a frontier release.
items.author: GuangyuRobert

items.title: Spec-interview first, then autonomy on week-sized tasks
items.why_useful: The emerging workflow: pick a backlog item you would normally scope for a full week, let Fable 5 interview you to build the spec, then hand it the work. Others split it into three explicit workflows: plan, code, review (performance, security, conventions).
items.author: shamshudein

items.title: goal-prompt-coach: a plugin that turns rough ideas into self-verifying prompts
items.why_useful: A Claude Code plugin tuned for Fable 5: give it a rough idea, it asks a few questions, then hands back a /goal prompt the model runs solo until the work checks out. A small, copyable pattern for anyone building prompt-coaching workflows.
items.author: dontcallmejames

items.title: Curated component libraries as working context for design tasks
items.why_useful: Electrical panel design by giving Fable 5 access to a component library, a BOM, a skill library and a few heuristics: 'far from perfect, but already better than some layout engines'. The general pattern: curated domain libraries beat raw prompting for structured design work.
items.author: K_leeeb

items.title: Text to rendered explainer videos with Claude Code + Remotion
items.why_useful: Pairing Claude Code with Remotion to turn plain text into fully rendered motion-graphics explainer videos, with Fable 5 writing the components. A deterministic, lock-in-free video pipeline for anyone producing educational content.
items.author: farxxxxx1
items.claimed_metrics: $14,600/month by a dev using this stack (self-reported by author)
